cards.q: Define immunization, and explain the difference between passive and active immunization.
cards.lecturer: Immunization is defined as the procedure by which the body is prepared to fight against specific disease. It is used to induce the immune resistance of the body to a specific disease. Immunization is of two types: passive immunization and active immunization. Passive immunization is produced without challenging the immune system of the body; it is produced by administration of serum or gamma globulins from a person who is already immunized to a non-immune person. Active immunization is acquired by activating the immune system of the body; the body develops resistance against disease by producing antibodies following the exposure to antigens.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Read the definition slowly

>> The procedure by which the body is **prepared** to fight against **specific** disease.

Two words are doing all the work.

---

**"Prepared"** — this happens **before** the disease arrives. Immunization is not treatment. It is preparation.

**"Specific"** — it protects against **one named disease**. Measles vaccine does nothing for polio.

---

## Step 2: The one question that separates the two types

>> **Does the body do the work, or is it handed the finished product?**

---

## Step 3: PASSIVE — you are given the answer

The key phrase:

>> **"Produced WITHOUT challenging the immune system of the body."**

---

**What does "challenging" mean?**

Exposing the immune system to an antigen and making it respond.

>> In passive immunization, that **never happens**. The body's immune system is completely uninvolved.

---

**Think of it like this:**

Someone else already fought the battle and made the antibodies. You are simply **given** those antibodies.

---

**Consequences follow immediately:**

- **Works straight away** — the antibody is already made
- **Does not last** — antibodies are proteins, and they break down
- **No memory** — your body learnt nothing, so it cannot make more

---

## Step 4: ACTIVE — you work it out yourself

>> "Acquired by **activating** the immune system of the body... **producing antibodies** following exposure to antigens."

---

**Your own immune system:**

- Sees the antigen
- Recognises it as foreign
- Makes antibodies against it
- **Creates memory cells**

---

**Consequences:**

- **Slow to start** — takes days to weeks
- **Long lasting**
- **Has memory** — meet the real disease later and the response is fast and huge

---

## Step 5: The comparison that matters clinically

| | **PASSIVE** | **ACTIVE** |
|---|---|---|
| Who makes the antibody | **Someone else** | **You** |
| Immune system involved | **No** | **Yes** |
| Onset | **Immediate** | Slow |
| Duration | Short | **Long** |
| Memory | **None** | **Yes** |

---

## Step 6: Why does this decide what you give a patient?

This is the practical payoff.

---

**A child stands on a dirty nail today, and has never been immunised.**

- He needs protection **now** → **passive** (tetanus immunoglobulin)
- He also needs protection **for the future** → **active** (tetanus toxoid)

>> **You give BOTH — at the same visit, in different sites.** They do completely different jobs.

That is exactly why the neonatal tetanus lecture says to give **antitoxin AND the DT vaccine**.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Passive = borrowed antibody. Immediate, temporary, no memory.**

>> **Active = your own antibody. Slow, lasting, with memory.**

And the reason both exist is that neither one alone covers both *now* and *later*.
cards.tie:
This distinction runs through the whole lecture.

Every immune globulin is **passive**.

Every vaccine and toxoid is **active**.

Get this right and the rest of the topic organises itself.

cards.q: Explain natural and artificial passive immunization, and why maternal antibody matters.
cards.lecturer: Passive immunization is acquired either naturally or artificially. Passive natural immunization is acquired from the mother before and after birth. Before birth, immunity is transferred from mother to the fetus in the form of maternal antibodies (mainly IgG) through the placenta. After birth, the antibodies (IgA) are transferred through breast milk. Passive artificial immunization is developed by injecting previously preformed antibodies using serum from humans or animals. This type of immunity is useful for providing immediate protection against acute infections like tetanus, measles, etc.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Nature invented passive immunization first

Every baby is born with borrowed antibodies. This is not a medical intervention — it is normal physiology.

---

## Step 2: Before birth — IgG across the placenta

>> **Mainly IgG**, transferred **through the placenta**.

---

**Why IgG and not the others?**

>> **IgG is the ONLY antibody class that crosses the placenta.**

There is an active transport system in the placenta that specifically carries IgG from mother to fetus.

---

**What does this achieve?**

The baby is **born already protected** against everything the mother is immune to.

>> And this is precisely why **maternal tetanus toxoid protects the newborn** — the mother makes IgG, the IgG crosses, and the baby has protection on day one.

It is also why **two doses** of toxoid are needed: the second dose produces the strong **IgG** secondary response.

---

## Step 3: After birth — IgA in breast milk

>> **IgA** transferred through **breast milk**.

---

**Why does the class change after birth?**

Because the job has changed.

---

**Before birth:** the threat is in the **blood**. IgG works in blood.

**After birth:** the threat is arriving through the **gut** — swallowed organisms.

>> **IgA is the mucosal antibody.** It coats the lining of the gut and stops organisms attaching.

---

>> This is why breastfeeding protects against diarrhoeal disease. The IgA lines the baby's intestine like a shield.

And it connects to a card later in the lecture: **antiadhesins prevent adhesion to mucosal surfaces** — that is exactly what IgA does.

---

## Step 4: The critical consequence — the window of vulnerability

This is the clinically important part.

---

**Maternal IgG does not last.**

It is broken down over the first months of life.

---

**So there is a gap:**

- Maternal antibody is **falling**
- The baby's own immunity is **not yet built**

>> That window is when the baby is most vulnerable — and it is **why the immunization schedule starts so early**.

---

## Step 5: And maternal antibody causes a problem too

Go forward in the lecture to live attenuated vaccines:

>> **"Circulating antibody may interfere with response."**

---

**Why?**

**Step 1:** You give a **live** vaccine — the organism must **replicate** to stimulate immunity

**Step 2:** But maternal antibody is still circulating

**Step 3:** It **binds and neutralises** the vaccine organism

**Step 4:** The organism cannot replicate

**Step 5:** **The vaccine fails**

---

>> This is why **measles vaccine is given at 9 months, not at birth.** You must wait for maternal antibody to fall enough.

**Maternal antibody is both the baby's protection and the vaccine's enemy.**

It also appears as a **host factor** later: *pre-existing antibodies* affect the immune response.

---

## Step 6: Passive ARTIFICIAL — when we do it deliberately

>> **Injecting previously preformed antibodies**, using **serum from humans or animals**.

---

**The stated purpose:**

>> **"Immediate protection against acute infections like tetanus, measles."**

**"Immediate"** is the whole point.

---

**When do you need immediate protection?**

- The child is **already exposed**
- Or the disease is **already developing**
- And there is **no time** to wait weeks for active immunity

---

>> A dirty wound today cannot wait three weeks for a vaccine to work. That is what antitoxin is for.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **IgG crosses the placenta. IgA comes in breast milk.**

>> Maternal antibody **protects the baby** — and **blocks live vaccines**.

>> Artificial passive immunization exists for one reason: **when you need protection today.**
cards.tie:
This card connects to three others:

- Live vaccines — circulating antibody interferes
- Host factors — pre-existing antibodies
- Immune globulins — the artificial form in practice

cards.q: Compare live attenuated and inactivated vaccines. Why do their properties differ?
cards.lecturer: Live attenuated vaccines are produced by modifying a naturally occurring (wild) organism, usually by repeated culturing. They retain the ability to replicate and produce an immune response similar to natural infection. Circulating antibody may interfere with response. They give long lasting immunity and one dose usually suffices. Inactivated vaccines: the organism is inactivated with heat and/or chemicals (formalin); with fractional vaccines it is further treated to purify only those components to be included in the vaccine. They are non-replicating so cannot cause disease, there is less interference from circulating antibodies, they require more doses (3–5), produce mostly humoral immune response, and antibody titres diminish with time.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: One difference explains all the others

>> **Live vaccines REPLICATE. Inactivated vaccines DO NOT.**

Hold that single fact and every other difference falls out of it. You do not need to memorise the table.

---

## Step 2: What does "attenuated" mean?

**Attenuate** = to weaken.

>> The wild organism is **modified by repeated culturing** — grown over and over in the laboratory until it loses its ability to cause disease, but keeps its ability to replicate.

It is still **alive**. It just cannot make you ill.

---

## Step 3: Now derive every property of live vaccines

---

**"Produces an immune response SIMILAR TO NATURAL INFECTION"**

**Why?** Because it replicates — exactly as the real organism would.

>> The immune system cannot tell the difference. It mounts the full response: antibodies **and** T-cells.

---

**"ONE DOSE usually suffices"**

**Why?** Because the organism multiplies inside you.

>> One dose becomes thousands of organisms. The dose amplifies itself.

---

**"LONG LASTING immunity"**

**Why?** Because the response mimics natural infection — and natural infection usually gives lifelong immunity.

---

**"Circulating antibody MAY INTERFERE with response"**

**Why?** This is the weakness, and it follows from the same fact.

>> The organism **must replicate** to work. Circulating antibody neutralises it before it can. **The vaccine fails.**

This is why measles vaccine is delayed until maternal antibody has fallen.

---

## Step 4: Now derive every property of inactivated vaccines

---

**"CANNOT cause disease"**

**Why?** It is dead. It cannot replicate, so it cannot cause infection under any circumstances.

>> **This is the great safety advantage** — and it is why these are the vaccines you can give to immunocompromised children.

---

**"Requires MORE doses — 3 to 5"**

**Why?** No replication means no amplification.

>> What you inject is all the antigen there will ever be. So you must inject repeatedly to build a response.

---

**"Antibody titres DIMINISH with time"**

**Why?** The stimulus was brief and never repeated itself.

>> Hence **boosters**.

---

**"Mostly HUMORAL immune response"**

**Humoral** = antibody-mediated.

**Why not cellular?** Because cell-mediated immunity is best stimulated by organisms **inside** cells — and a dead organism never gets inside a cell.

---

**"LESS interference from circulating antibodies"**

**Why?** It does not need to replicate, so neutralising some of it does not defeat it.

---

## Step 5: The complete picture

| | **LIVE** | **INACTIVATED** |
|---|---|---|
| Replicates | **Yes** | No |
| Response | Like natural infection | **Mostly humoral** |
| Doses | **One** | **3–5** |
| Duration | **Long** | Diminishes |
| Antibody interference | **Yes** | Less |
| Can cause disease | Possible in immunocompromised | **Never** |

---

## Step 6: The one clinical rule that follows

>> **Live vaccines are generally NOT given to immunocompromised children.**

---

**Why?** Because "attenuated" means weakened **relative to a normal immune system**.

>> In a child with no immune defence, a weakened organism can still replicate freely and cause the disease.

This is exactly why the lecturer says **BCG and yellow fever are contraindicated in symptomatic HIV**, and why **IPV (inactivated) is preferred to OPV (live)**.

---

## Step 7: How to remember which is which

**LIVE ATTENUATED — viruses:** polio (oral), measles, rubella, mumps, rabies, varicella, yellow fever, rotavirus, influenza

**LIVE ATTENUATED — bacteria:** **BCG**, typhoid

---

**INACTIVATED — viral:** polio (**Salk**), hepatitis A, rabies, influenza

**INACTIVATED — bacterial:** pertussis, typhoid, cholera, plague, anthrax

---

>> Notice that **polio, typhoid, rabies and influenza appear in BOTH lists.** They exist in both live and killed forms.

That is why you must specify: **OPV (live) versus IPV/Salk (inactivated)**.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Replication is the whole story.**

It gives live vaccines their strength (one dose, lifelong, natural-type response), their weakness (antibody interference), and their danger (immunocompromised children).
cards.tie:
This card explains the HIV recommendations, the contraindications list, and why the schedule places measles at 9 months.

Three separate parts of the lecture, one underlying mechanism.

cards.q: Describe the fractional vaccines and the four types of immune globulin.
cards.lecturer: Fractional vaccines: subunit (made from a single protein) — hepatitis B, acellular pertussis, human papilloma virus; toxoids — diphtheria, tetanus; polysaccharide — pneumococcal, meningococcal, Salmonella typhi; conjugate polysaccharide — Hib, pneumococcal, meningococcal; recombinant (from recombinant DNA technology) — hepatitis B, human papilloma virus, influenza. Immune globulins: immune globulin — homologous pooled antibody from many adults, containing antibodies to many antigens, for hepatitis A and measles; homologous human hyperimmune globulin — from humans with high levels of specific antibodies, for PEP of hepatitis B, rabies, tetanus, varicella; heterologous hyperimmune serum (antitoxin) — from animals, for botulism and diphtheria; monoclonal antibodies derived from a clone of antibody-producing cells — RSV, palivizumab.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: What is a "fractional" vaccine?

>> A **fraction** = a **part** of the organism, not the whole thing.

The organism is inactivated and then **further treated to purify only those components to be included**.

---

**Why bother?**

>> Because most of the organism is **useless antigen that causes side effects** without adding protection.

Take only the part the immune system needs to recognise, and you get **fewer adverse reactions**.

---

**The best example is in the list:** **acellular pertussis**.

>> Whole-cell pertussis caused more reactions. The **acellular** version uses only selected proteins — and is far better tolerated.

---

## Step 2: TOXOIDS — the cleverest idea in vaccinology

**Diphtheria and tetanus.**

---

**Ask the key question: what actually harms you in tetanus?**

>> **Not the bacterium. The TOXIN.**

*C. tetani* never leaves the wound. Tetanospasmin does all the damage.

---

**So immunise against the toxin, not the organism.**

**A toxoid** = the toxin, chemically treated so it can no longer do damage — but still **looks the same to the immune system**.

---

>> Your body makes antibodies against the shape. Real toxin arrives later, is bound and neutralised, and never reaches the nerve.

**Same logic for diphtheria** — a toxin-mediated disease.

>> **Toxin-mediated disease → toxoid vaccine.** That is the rule.

---

## Step 3: POLYSACCHARIDE — and the problem with it

**Pneumococcal, meningococcal, *Salmonella typhi*.**

---

These organisms have a **polysaccharide (sugar) capsule** that hides them from phagocytes. So the capsule is the target.

---

**But there is a serious problem:**

>> Polysaccharide antigens produce a **weak response in young children — especially under 2 years — and generate poor memory.**

Sugars do not engage T-cells well, and T-cell help is what creates memory.

---

## Step 4: CONJUGATE POLYSACCHARIDE — the solution

**Hib, pneumococcal, meningococcal.**

---

**What is done?**

>> The polysaccharide is **chemically linked (conjugated) to a PROTEIN**.

---

**Why does that fix it?**

- Proteins **do** engage T-cells
- T-cell help converts a weak, memory-free response into a **strong response with memory**
- And it now **works in infants**

---

>> **This is why Hib disease collapsed after conjugate vaccines were introduced.** The plain polysaccharide version simply did not protect the babies who needed it most.

Note that pneumococcal and meningococcal appear in **both** lists — plain and conjugate versions exist.

---

## Step 5: SUBUNIT and RECOMBINANT

**Subunit** = made from a **single protein**. Hepatitis B, acellular pertussis, HPV.

**Recombinant** = made using **recombinant DNA technology**. Hepatitis B, HPV, influenza.

---

>> Notice hepatitis B and HPV appear in **both** — because the single protein is **manufactured** by recombinant DNA technology rather than extracted from the virus.

**Subunit describes what it is. Recombinant describes how it was made.**

---

## Step 6: Immune globulins — all PASSIVE

Every item here is **preformed antibody**. Immediate protection, no memory.

---

**Sort them by two questions: how specific, and from whom?**

---

### 1. Immune globulin

**Homologous pooled antibody, from MANY adults.**

>> "Homologous" = from the **same species** — human.

Contains antibodies to **many antigens**, because it is pooled from many people.

**Used for: hepatitis A, measles.**

>> **Broad but dilute** — it contains a bit of everything.

---

### 2. Homologous human hyperimmune globulin

**From humans with HIGH levels of SPECIFIC antibody.**

>> "Hyper" = high. Donors are deliberately selected for high titres against one disease.

**Used for PEP: hepatitis B, rabies, tetanus, varicella.**

>> **Narrow but concentrated.** This is what TIG is in the tetanus lecture.

---

### 3. Heterologous hyperimmune serum (antitoxin)

**From ANIMALS.**

>> "Heterologous" = from a **different species** — usually horse.

**Used for: botulism, diphtheria.**

---

>> **This is the dangerous one.** Animal protein triggers **anaphylaxis and serum sickness**.

Which is exactly why the tetanus lecture says to give TAT only *"assuming sensitivity reactions to horse serum are negative"*, and why **human TIG is always preferred**.

---

### 4. Monoclonal antibodies

**Derived from a CLONE of antibody-producing cells.**

>> A clone = identical cells → **identical antibodies**, all against one exact target.

**Used for: RSV — palivizumab.**

>> The most precise of all — but expensive.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Toxin disease → toxoid.**

>> **Sugar capsule → conjugate it to protein so infants respond.**

>> **Immune globulins: broad pooled → specific human → animal (risky) → monoclonal (precise).**
cards.tie:
The immune globulin section is the passive immunization card in practice.

And 'heterologous = animal = anaphylaxis' explains the horse serum warning you met in neonatal tetanus.

cards.q: What determines the immune response to a vaccine, and what are the classes of protective antibody?
cards.lecturer: Determinants of immune response: chemical and physical state of the vaccine; route/site of administration; vaccine dose; immunogenicity of the vaccine. Host factors: age, sex, nutrition, pre-existing antibodies, genetics, immune status, pregnancy. Classes of protective antibody: antitoxins inactivate soluble toxic protein products; opsonins facilitate phagocytosis; lysins interact with components of serum complement; neutralising antibodies prevent proliferation of infectious virus; antiadhesins prevent adhesion to mucosal surfaces.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Two sides to every vaccination

>> **The vaccine** brings four variables. **The host** brings seven.

A vaccine does not work by itself. It works **on somebody**.

---

## Step 2: The vaccine factors

---

**Chemical and physical state**

Live or killed, whole or fractional, with or without adjuvant.

>> This is the previous card in one line — and it determines how many doses and how long protection lasts.

---

**Route/site of administration**

This matters more than students expect.

>> **BCG must be intradermal.** Give it subcutaneously and you get an abscess instead of protection.

>> **Hepatitis B in the deltoid or thigh, not the buttock** — fat absorbs poorly and the response is weaker.

**Oral polio** works on gut mucosa, producing local **IgA** that injected polio cannot.

---

**Vaccine dose**

Too little → inadequate stimulus. The dose is fixed by trials, not by the child's weight.

---

**Immunogenicity**

How good the antigen is at provoking a response.

>> Remember: **plain polysaccharide is poorly immunogenic in infants** — hence conjugation.

---

## Step 3: The host factors — and why each one matters

---

**AGE**

The most important of the seven.

- **Newborns** — immature immune system, plus maternal antibody interfering
- **Infants under 2** — respond poorly to plain polysaccharides
- **Elderly** — declining responses

>> This is why the schedule exists at all. Timing is not administrative — it is immunological.

---

**NUTRITION**

>> A malnourished child mounts a **weaker response** to the same vaccine.

Protein-energy malnutrition impairs both antibody production and cell-mediated immunity.

**Hugely relevant here.** And note it is **not** a contraindication — you still immunise. But you may not get full protection.

---

**PRE-EXISTING ANTIBODIES**

>> Maternal antibody, or antibody from a recent transfusion or immunoglobulin, **neutralises live vaccine before it can replicate**.

This is why **blood transfusion is a precaution for MMR** later in the lecture — it is the same mechanism appearing again.

---

**IMMUNE STATUS**

HIV, malignancy, steroids, immunosuppressants.

>> Two problems: the response may be **poor**, and **live** vaccines may be **dangerous**.

---

**GENETICS**

Some people are simply non-responders — the classic example is hepatitis B vaccine non-response.

---

**SEX and PREGNANCY**

Pregnancy alters immune function and is a **contraindication** to live vaccines.

---

## Step 4: The five classes of protective antibody

>> Antibodies do not all work the same way. **Each class disables the pathogen at a different point.**

Learn them by **what they stop**.

---

### ANTITOXINS

>> **Inactivate soluble toxic protein products.**

They bind the **toxin**, not the organism.

**Relevant to:** tetanus, diphtheria, botulism.

>> This is why **toxoid vaccines** work — they generate antitoxins.

---

### OPSONINS

>> **Facilitate phagocytosis.**

*Opsonin* comes from a Greek word meaning "to prepare food for eating".

---

**How it works:** the antibody **coats the organism**, giving phagocytes something to grip.

>> Encapsulated bacteria — pneumococcus, meningococcus, Hib — are **slippery**. Phagocytes cannot grasp the capsule.

**Opsonins make them grippable.** This is exactly how the capsular vaccines protect.

---

### LYSINS

>> **Interact with components of serum complement.**

The antibody binds the organism and **activates complement**, which punches holes in the bacterial membrane and lyses it.

---

### NEUTRALISING ANTIBODIES

>> **Prevent proliferation of infectious virus.**

They block the virus from **entering cells**. A virus outside a cell cannot replicate.

**Relevant to:** measles, polio, all viral vaccines.

---

### ANTIADHESINS

>> **Prevent adhesion to mucosal surfaces.**

Most infections begin by **sticking** to a mucosal surface. Block the sticking and the infection never starts.

---

>> This is what **IgA in breast milk** does — and it is why oral polio vaccine, given at the mucosal surface, produces protection that injected vaccine does not.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Five classes, five points of attack:**

- **Antitoxin** — stops the poison
- **Opsonin** — makes it edible
- **Lysin** — bursts it open
- **Neutralising** — stops it entering cells
- **Antiadhesin** — stops it sticking in the first place

>> And whichever one you need, the **host** must be well enough to make it.
cards.tie:
The host factors explain why the same vaccine gives different protection in different children.

And the antibody classes explain the design logic behind toxoids, conjugates and oral vaccines.

cards.q: What are the objectives of an immunization programme, and what is herd immunity?
cards.lecturer: Objectives of the immunization programme: not just individual protection; decrease morbidity and mortality from target diseases; increase herd immunity — the resistance of a group to invasion and spread of an infectious agent due to immunity of a large proportion of the group; high coverage is needed to achieve this. Vaccine recommendation factors to consider: epidemiology (incidence, age, sex); age-specific morbidity and mortality; risk of adverse events; cost effectiveness; age of recommended health care visits; efficacy of the vaccine. Vaccinations are generally recommended at the age of significant risk of disease and complications, and protective immunologic response.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: The first line reframes everything

>> **"NOT JUST individual protection."**

This is the sentence that separates immunization from every other medical intervention.

---

**When you treat a child's pneumonia, you help that child.**

>> **When you immunise a child, you also help every child he would have infected.**

That is a genuinely different kind of medicine.

---

## Step 2: What is herd immunity, exactly?

>> **The resistance of a GROUP to invasion and spread of an infectious agent, due to immunity of a LARGE PROPORTION of the group.**

---

**Two words are essential:**

**GROUP** — herd immunity is a property of a **population**, not a person. No individual has herd immunity.

**LARGE PROPORTION** — it only appears once **enough** people are immune.

---

## Step 3: How does it actually work? Think about the chain.

---

**An infectious disease spreads person to person, like a chain:**

Infected person → susceptible person → susceptible person → and on

---

**Now immunise most of the population.**

The infected person now meets mostly **immune** people.

>> The chain **breaks**, because there is nowhere for the organism to go next.

---

>> **Immune people are not just protected. They are ROADBLOCKS.**

That is the mechanism in one image.

---

## Step 4: Who does this protect that vaccination alone cannot?

This is why herd immunity matters so much.

---

**Some people cannot be vaccinated:**

- **Newborns**, too young for the schedule
- **Immunocompromised** children — live vaccines contraindicated
- Children with **true anaphylaxis** to a vaccine
- Children who **failed** to respond (genetic non-responders, malnutrition)

---

>> These children are protected **only** by the immunity of everyone around them.

**The child who cannot be vaccinated is protected by the child who was.**

---

## Step 5: Why does the lecturer stress "HIGH COVERAGE"?

Because herd immunity has a **threshold**.

---

**Below the threshold** — chains of transmission continue, and outbreaks occur

**Above it** — transmission collapses

---

>> This is why **coverage** gets its own slide, and why **missed opportunities** matter so much.

Every unimmunised child is a gap in the wall. Enough gaps, and the wall fails.

And measles — highly infectious — needs a **very** high threshold.

---

## Step 6: How is a vaccine chosen for a programme?

Six factors, and they are not all medical.

---

**Epidemiology — incidence, age, sex**

>> Is the disease actually common **here**? A vaccine for a disease we do not have is wasted money.

---

**Age-specific morbidity and mortality**

>> **At what age does this disease kill?** That determines when to vaccinate.

---

**Risk of adverse events**

The vaccine must be safer than the disease.

---

**Cost effectiveness**

National programmes have finite budgets. Money spent here is not spent there.

---

**Age of recommended health care visits**

>> Purely practical, and often decisive. Schedule the vaccine for a day the child is **already coming** — and coverage rises.

Schedule it separately, and many will not come.

---

**Efficacy of the vaccine**

Does it actually work?

---

## Step 7: The timing rule — and its built-in tension

>> Vaccinate at the age of **significant risk of disease and complications**, AND at the age of **protective immunologic response**.

---

**These two pull in opposite directions:**

- **Risk says: give it EARLY** — before the child is exposed
- **Immunology says: give it LATER** — when he can actually respond

---

>> **The schedule is the compromise between those two forces.**

Measles at 9 months is exactly this: early enough to protect before the peak risk, late enough for maternal antibody to have fallen so the live vaccine can replicate.

**Now you know why the schedule looks the way it does.**

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Immunization protects the child, and it protects the community.**

>> **Herd immunity breaks chains of transmission** — and it is the only protection available to those who cannot be vaccinated.

>> **It requires high coverage** — which is why every missed opportunity counts.
cards.tie:
This explains why the lecture goes on to cover **coverage**, **missed opportunities** and the **cold chain**.

They all exist to serve one goal: get enough children immunised, with vaccines that still work.

cards.q: Distinguish contraindications, precautions and things that are NOT contraindications to vaccination.
cards.lecturer: Contraindications: anaphylaxis; encephalopathy; progressive neurologic disorders; immunodeficiency disorder and immunosuppressive therapy; pregnancy; any illness requiring admission to hospital — defer immunization. Precautions: fever above 40.5 °C; collapse/shock; inconsolable crying; Guillain–Barré syndrome; and for MMR, blood transfusion and thrombocytopenia. NOT contraindications: URTI; mild fever; diarrhoea — repeat dose; cerebral palsy; breast feeding; antimicrobial therapy.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Understand the three categories before the lists

>> **CONTRAINDICATION** = **do NOT give.** The risk outweighs the benefit.

>> **PRECAUTION** = **think carefully.** There may be increased risk, but the benefit may still win.

>> **NOT A CONTRAINDICATION** = **give the vaccine.** No reason to withhold.

---

>> The third list is arguably the **most important in the whole lecture** — because that is where children are wrongly turned away.

---

## Step 2: The contraindications, one by one

---

**ANAPHYLAXIS**

A severe, life-threatening allergic reaction to a previous dose or a vaccine component.

>> Give it again and you may kill the child. **This is absolute.**

---

**ENCEPHALOPATHY**

Brain dysfunction following a previous dose.

---

**PROGRESSIVE NEUROLOGIC DISORDERS**

>> Note the word **progressive** — a condition that is actively getting worse.

**Why withhold?** Because if the child deteriorates after vaccination, you cannot tell whether the vaccine or the disease caused it. So you wait until the condition is stable.

---

>> **Now contrast with CEREBRAL PALSY, which is on the NOT-contraindicated list.**

**Why the difference?**

>> Cerebral palsy is **static** — the brain injury already happened and is not advancing.

**Progressive = defer. Static = vaccinate.** That is the distinction being tested.

---

**IMMUNODEFICIENCY DISORDER / IMMUNOSUPPRESSIVE THERAPY**

>> This applies mainly to **LIVE** vaccines.

"Attenuated" means weakened **relative to a normal immune system**. In a child with no defences, the weakened organism can replicate freely and cause disease.

---

**PREGNANCY**

Again, chiefly live vaccines — risk to the fetus.

---

**ANY ILLNESS REQUIRING ADMISSION TO HOSPITAL — DEFER**

>> Note the word: **defer**, not cancel.

**Why?** If the child deteriorates, you will not know whether it was the illness or the vaccine. And a seriously ill child may respond poorly.

>> **Defer means you MUST come back to it.** A deferred vaccine that is never given becomes a missed opportunity.

---

## Step 3: The precautions — why are these different?

Each one is a **serious event following a previous dose** — worrying, but not proven to be an absolute danger.

---

**Fever above 40.5 °C** after a previous dose

**Collapse or shock** after a previous dose

**Inconsolable crying** after a previous dose

**Guillain–Barré syndrome**

---

>> These require **judgement**: how severe was it, how dangerous is the disease being prevented, how likely is exposure?

**A precaution says "weigh it up", not "never".**

---

## Step 4: The two MMR precautions — and why they are there

---

**BLOOD TRANSFUSION**

>> **Why?** Transfused blood contains **antibodies**.

MMR is a **live** vaccine. It must **replicate** to work.

>> Those transfused antibodies will **neutralise the vaccine virus** before it can replicate — and **the vaccine fails**.

This is exactly the same mechanism as maternal antibody blocking measles vaccine. **Pre-existing antibodies** appeared in the host factors card too.

**The concern is vaccine failure, not vaccine harm.**

---

**THROMBOCYTOPENIA**

MMR can cause a transient fall in platelets, which matters in a child whose platelets are already low.

---

## Step 5: The NOT-contraindications — learn these best of all

>> Every item on this list represents children who are **wrongly sent away** from clinics every day.

---

**URTI** — a runny nose is not a reason. This is probably the commonest wrongful refusal of all.

**MILD fever** — note the contrast: mild fever now = fine; fever **>40.5 °C after a previous dose** = precaution.

**CEREBRAL PALSY** — static, not progressive. **Vaccinate.**

**BREASTFEEDING** — no vaccine is contraindicated in a breastfeeding mother or baby.

**ANTIMICROBIAL THERAPY** — antibiotics do not interfere with vaccine response.

---

**DIARRHOEA — and note the extra instruction: "repeat dose"**

>> **Why repeat?** Because of **oral polio vaccine**.

OPV is swallowed and must be **taken up by the gut mucosa** to work. In diarrhoea, gut transit is fast and the vaccine may be flushed through before it is absorbed.

---

>> So: **give it anyway** — it may work — **but repeat the dose later** in case it did not.

**Do not withhold. Give and repeat.**

---

## Step 6: Why does this list matter so much?

Go forward to the **missed opportunity** slide:

>> A missed opportunity is when a child **with no contraindication** does not receive a vaccine he is **eligible for**.

---

>> Every child turned away for a runny nose or a mild fever **is a missed opportunity** — and a gap in herd immunity.

The not-contraindicated list is how you prevent them.

---

## Putting it all together

| | Action |
|---|---|
| **Contraindication** | **Do not give** |
| **Precaution** | **Weigh benefit against risk** |
| **Not a contraindication** | **GIVE IT** |

>> **Anaphylaxis, encephalopathy, progressive neuro, immunodeficiency, pregnancy, hospital illness → do not give.**

>> **URTI, mild fever, diarrhoea, cerebral palsy, breastfeeding, antibiotics → GIVE IT.**
cards.tie:
The contrast between **progressive** neurologic disorder and **cerebral palsy** is the hinge of this card.

One is advancing, one is static.

Same system, opposite decisions.

cards.q: What is the cold chain, and what are its rules?
cards.lecturer: Cold chain is a system of storage and transport of vaccines at low temperatures by the manufacturer to the actual vaccination site. The cold chain system is necessary because vaccine failure may occur due to failure of storage and transport under strict temperature controls. Vaccines stored in the freezer compartment are polio and measles. Vaccines which must be stored in the cold compartment and never allowed to freeze are BCG, DPT, DT, TT, typhoid and diluents. Vaccines must be protected from sunlight and prevented from contact with antiseptics. At health centres most vaccines (except polio) can be stored up to 5 weeks if refrigerator temperature is strictly kept between 4 °C and 8 °C. Storage of opened vial: with preservatives up to 3 hours; without preservatives up to 1 hour. Cold chain equipment: walk in cold rooms, deep freezer, small deep freezer, cold boxes, vaccine carrier, day carrier, ice packs.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Why is it called a CHAIN?

Because it is a **continuous, unbroken sequence**:

>> Manufacturer → national store → state store → health centre → vaccine carrier → the child's arm

---

**And like any chain, it is only as strong as its weakest link.**

>> One warm afternoon in a broken refrigerator, anywhere along that line, and the vaccine is dead. **It will still look normal. You will still inject it. The child will be unprotected.**

---

>> This is the frightening part: **a failed vaccine looks exactly like a working one.**

The child gets a card saying he is immunised. He is not.

---

## Step 2: Why are vaccines so fragile?

**For LIVE vaccines**

>> They contain **living organisms**. Heat kills them.

**A dead live-vaccine is not a killed vaccine — it is nothing at all.** It cannot replicate, so it cannot immunise.

---

**For INACTIVATED vaccines and toxoids**

>> These contain **proteins**. Freezing **denatures** protein and destroys the **adjuvant**.

Once the antigen's shape is destroyed, the immune system no longer recognises it.

---

## Step 3: THE SPLIT — and now it makes sense

---

**FREEZER compartment: POLIO and MEASLES**

>> Both are **live viral** vaccines. Freezing preserves them.

---

**COLD compartment — NEVER FREEZE: BCG, DPT, DT, TT, typhoid, and DILUENTS**

>> These are **inactivated vaccines, toxoids and adsorbed vaccines** — protein-based.

**Freezing destroys them.**

---

>> **Memory hook: the two live VIRAL ones go in the freezer. Everything else must stay cold but never frozen.**

---

**And why DILUENTS?**

This is the detail people forget.

>> A frozen diluent can **crack the vial**, and adding an ice-cold diluent to a freeze-dried vaccine can **damage it by thermal shock**.

**The diluent must be cooled to the same temperature as the vaccine it will reconstitute.**

---

## Step 4: Two other enemies

**SUNLIGHT**

>> **UV light destroys vaccines** — BCG and measles are particularly light-sensitive.

So keep them in their boxes, and never leave a vial on a sunny windowsill.

---

**ANTISEPTICS**

>> Antiseptics **kill living organisms** — which is exactly what a live vaccine is.

If you swab the skin, **let it dry** before injecting. Do not let antiseptic contaminate the needle, syringe or vial.

---

## Step 5: The numbers — learn them exactly

---

**Refrigerator: strictly 4 °C to 8 °C**

Not "cold". Not "chilled". **4–8 °C.**

>> Above 8 → live vaccines die. Below 0 → protein vaccines are destroyed.

**The window is narrow, and both ends are dangerous.**

---

**Health centre storage: most vaccines up to 5 WEEKS — except polio**

>> Polio is the **most heat-sensitive** of all, so it cannot be held as long.

---

**Opened vials:**

| | Time |
|---|---|
| **WITH preservative** | **3 hours** |
| **WITHOUT preservative** | **1 hour** |

---

**Why the difference?**

>> Once a vial is opened, **bacteria can enter** each time a needle goes in.

**A preservative slows bacterial growth**, so you get 3 hours. Without one, contamination is rapid — only 1 hour.

>> Use it after that and you may inject **bacteria** into a child.

---

## Step 6: The equipment — organise it by scale

**Largest to smallest:**

- **Walk-in cold rooms** — national and state level, huge volumes
- **Deep freezer** — for the frozen vaccines
- **Small deep freezer**
- **Cold boxes** — transport between facilities
- **Vaccine carrier** — carried to an outreach session
- **Day carrier** — a single day's supply
- **Ice packs** — keep the carriers cold

---

>> Notice the **carriers**: they exist so vaccination can go to villages and outreach clinics.

**The cold chain is what makes outreach possible at all.**

---

## Step 7: Why is this a whole section of the lecture?

Because it links everything.

---

>> A perfect vaccine, given to the right child, at the right age, by a trained nurse — **is worthless if it was warm last Tuesday.**

And nobody will ever know.

---

>> **The cold chain is the difference between immunization coverage and immunization PROTECTION.** A high coverage figure means nothing if the vaccines were dead.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Polio and measles → freezer. Everything else → 4–8 °C, never frozen.**

>> **Protect from sunlight and antiseptics.**

>> **Opened vial: 3 hours with preservative, 1 hour without.**

>> **And remember: a dead vaccine looks exactly like a live one.**
cards.tie:
This section ties directly to coverage and herd immunity.

A broken cold chain produces children who are recorded as immunised, counted in coverage figures — and completely unprotected.

cards.q: CLINICAL SCENARIO 1 — A mother brings her 9-month-old for measles vaccination. He has a runny nose, a temperature of 37.8 °C and passed three loose stools today. He is feeding well and playing. The nurse wants to send them home to return when he is well. Is she right, and what do you do?
cards.lecturer:
**The nurse is wrong. Vaccinate the child today.**

**Basis:** Upper respiratory tract infection, mild fever and diarrhoea are explicitly listed as **NOT contraindications** to vaccination. Withholding the vaccine here would create a **missed opportunity** — which occurs when an individual in whom there is no contraindication does not receive the vaccine for which he is eligible when he visits a health facility. Among the listed causes of missed opportunities are **not checking immune status** and **failure to immunize every day**.

**Management:** give the measles vaccine today. Check the immunization card and give **all other vaccines for which he is due at the same visit**, since **failure to give simultaneous injections** is another listed cause of missed opportunity. If **oral polio vaccine** is given, note that in the presence of diarrhoea the **dose should be repeated**. Provide information to the parent on expected adverse reactions — pain, swelling, redness, soreness, fever and rash — since **failure to provide information to parents** is itself a cause of missed opportunities. Advise on management of the diarrhoea and review if it worsens.

**Contrast:** vaccination would only be deferred for an illness requiring **admission to hospital**, which this is not.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Check each finding against the three lists

This is exactly what the lists are for. Go through them one at a time.

---

**Runny nose = URTI**

>> On the **NOT a contraindication** list. **Vaccinate.**

---

**Temperature 37.8 °C = mild fever**

>> On the **NOT a contraindication** list. **Vaccinate.**

Note the contrast: the precaution is fever **above 40.5 °C** — and even that refers to fever **after a previous dose**, not fever today.

---

**Three loose stools = diarrhoea**

>> On the **NOT a contraindication** list — with one instruction attached: **repeat dose**.

---

>> **All three findings are explicitly listed as not contraindications.** There is no judgement call here. The answer is in the lecture.

---

## Step 2: What WOULD have justified deferring?

>> **"Any illness requiring admission to hospital — defer immunization."**

---

Is this child that ill?

- **Feeding well** ✓
- **Playing** ✓
- Mild fever only
- Three loose stools

---

>> He is a well child with a minor illness. Nowhere near admission.

**Defer means seriously ill — not slightly unwell.**

---

## Step 3: Why does the "repeat the dose" instruction exist for diarrhoea?

This is worth understanding rather than memorising.

---

**It is about ORAL polio vaccine.**

**Step 1:** OPV is swallowed

**Step 2:** It must be **taken up by the gut mucosa** to replicate and stimulate immunity

**Step 3:** In diarrhoea, gut transit is **fast**

**Step 4:** The vaccine may be flushed through before it is absorbed

---

>> So the instruction is **not** "withhold it". It is: **give it — it may work — and repeat it later in case it did not.**

**Give and repeat. Never withhold.**

(Note that measles vaccine is injected, so diarrhoea does not affect it at all.)

---

## Step 4: Why is turning him away so harmful?

Go to the missed opportunity definition:

>> A missed opportunity occurs when an individual **in whom there is no contraindication** does not receive the vaccine for which he is **eligible** when he **visits a health facility**.

---

**This case fits that definition exactly:**

- No contraindication ✓
- Eligible ✓
- Visiting a health facility ✓

---

**And what happens next in real life?**

- The mother may live far away
- She may have paid for transport
- She may have taken a day off work
- **She may not come back**

---

>> **You may have just cost this child his measles vaccine permanently.**

And measles is one of the most infectious diseases there is — so this also costs the community a piece of its **herd immunity**.

---

## Step 5: Why 9 months for measles?

Since this is the visit, understand why the age is what it is.

---

**Measles vaccine is LIVE ATTENUATED.**

>> A live vaccine must **replicate** to work. And **circulating antibody may interfere with response**.

---

**So:**

- **Too early** → maternal IgG is still present → it **neutralises the vaccine virus** → the vaccine fails
- **Too late** → the child is exposed to wild measles before he is protected

---

>> **9 months is the compromise** — late enough for maternal antibody to have fallen, early enough to protect before peak risk.

This is exactly the tension in the recommendation slide: **age of significant risk** versus **age of protective immunologic response**.

---

## Step 6: Do more than the one vaccine

Check the card and give **everything he is due**.

---

>> **"Failure to give simultaneous injections"** is a listed cause of missed opportunities.

Giving two or three vaccines at one visit is safe, effective, and saves another journey.

---

**And also check his immune status** — *"not checking immune status"* is another listed cause. He may have missed earlier doses entirely.

---

## Step 7: Talk to the mother

>> **"Failure to provide info to parents"** is a cause of missed opportunities in its own right.

---

Tell her what to expect: **pain, swelling, redness, soreness, fever, rash**.

>> A mother who is warned about fever manages it at home. A mother who is not, concludes the vaccine harmed her child — and does not bring the next one.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **URTI, mild fever and diarrhoea are NOT contraindications. Vaccinate today.**

>> Give everything he is due. Repeat the OPV dose. Tell the mother what to expect.

>> **The child in front of you is the one you can immunise. He may not come back.**
cards.tie:
This is the single most practically useful card in the topic.

The NOT-contraindicated list exists precisely because these children are turned away every day — and every one of them is a hole in herd immunity.

cards.q: CLINICAL SCENARIO 2 — A 6-week-old baby of an HIV-positive mother is brought for immunization. The baby's HIV status is unknown and he is asymptomatic and thriving. The health worker asks whether BCG, OPV and the other vaccines can be given. What is your advice?
cards.lecturer:
**Advice:** an **asymptomatic** child may receive **all vaccines**. BCG and yellow fever are contraindicated **only if symptomatic**. **IPV is preferred to OPV.**

**Basis:** in HIV, all vaccines may be given if the child is asymptomatic; **BCG and yellow fever are contraindicated if symptomatic**; and **IPV is preferred to OPV**. The general principle is that **live vaccines are generally not given to immunocompromised children**, because live attenuated organisms retain the ability to replicate and may cause disease where there is no effective immune response. Immunodeficiency disorder and immunosuppressive therapy are listed contraindications.

**Management:** give the vaccines due at 6 weeks. Use **IPV in preference to OPV**, as IPV is inactivated and non-replicating and therefore cannot cause vaccine-associated disease. Arrange **HIV testing** for the baby and follow up his clinical status, since the recommendation changes the moment he becomes symptomatic. Ensure the mother is on treatment and counselled about infant feeding. Also consider **pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine**, which is indicated in HIV among other special circumstances. Record everything, review at each visit, and avoid **missed opportunities**.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: The whole answer turns on ONE word

>> **ASYMPTOMATIC.**

---

The lecturer's rule is precise:

- **HIV — all vaccines if ASYMPTOMATIC**
- **BCG and yellow fever contraindicated if SYMPTOMATIC**
- **IPV preferred to OPV**

---

>> **HIV infection alone does not bar vaccination. Being SYMPTOMATIC does.**

This baby is asymptomatic and thriving. So he can be vaccinated.

---

## Step 2: Why does symptomatic status matter so much?

Because it is a proxy for **how badly the immune system is damaged**.

---

**Asymptomatic** → immune function is still reasonably preserved → he can control an attenuated organism

**Symptomatic** → immunity has failed → he **cannot** control even a weakened organism

---

>> **"Attenuated" means weakened relative to a NORMAL immune system.**

Give a live organism to a child with no defences, and it replicates freely — and causes the disease it was meant to prevent.

Hence: **live vaccines are generally not given to immunocompromised children.**

---

## Step 3: Why BCG and yellow fever specifically?

Because they are **live attenuated** — and BCG is a **live attenuated BACTERIUM**.

---

>> In a severely immunocompromised child, BCG can cause **disseminated BCG disease** — a serious, sometimes fatal infection.

Yellow fever is a live attenuated virus, with the same problem.

---

## Step 4: Why is IPV preferred to OPV? Understand this properly.

This is the highest-yield reasoning in the case.

---

**OPV** = **Oral Polio Vaccine** = **LIVE attenuated**

**IPV** = **Inactivated Polio Vaccine** (Salk) = **KILLED**

---

**The problem with OPV in this setting:**

**Step 1:** OPV contains live virus that **replicates in the gut**

**Step 2:** In an immunocompromised child, replication is **uncontrolled and prolonged**

**Step 3:** The virus can **revert towards virulence**

**Step 4:** Result: **vaccine-associated paralytic poliomyelitis**

---

>> **The vaccine causes the very disease it was designed to prevent.**

**IPV cannot do this.** It is non-replicating — so it **cannot cause disease** under any circumstances.

---

>> This is the live-versus-inactivated card being applied at the bedside. **Non-replicating = safe in immunodeficiency.**

---

## Step 5: Why does his HIV status being unknown not stop you?

Because the decision rests on **clinical status**, not on a test result.

---

- He is **asymptomatic** → all vaccines permitted
- Waiting for a test = **delay** = **missed opportunity** = an unprotected baby

---

>> **But test him.** Because the recommendation changes the moment he becomes symptomatic — and you cannot manage him going forward without knowing.

---

## Step 6: The extra vaccine most people forget

>> **Pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine** is indicated in **HIV** — alongside SCA, anatomic asplenia, nephrotic syndrome, renal failure and organ transplant.

---

**Why these conditions together?**

>> They all involve **poor defence against encapsulated organisms**.

Asplenia removes the organ that clears encapsulated bacteria. HIV impairs the immune response to them. Nephrotic syndrome loses immunoglobulin in the urine.

>> **Same vulnerability, different routes.** That is why one vaccine covers the whole list.

---

## Step 7: This is not a one-off decision

>> **Review his clinical status at every visit.**

He is asymptomatic **today**. If he becomes symptomatic, live vaccines must stop.

---

And the mother needs: treatment for herself, and counselling about infant feeding.

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Asymptomatic HIV → give all vaccines.**

>> **Symptomatic HIV → no BCG, no yellow fever.**

>> **Always prefer IPV to OPV — inactivated cannot cause disease.**

>> **Test him, review him, and add pneumococcal vaccine.**
cards.tie:
This case is the live-versus-inactivated distinction turned into a clinical decision.

And it shows why 'immunodeficiency' appears in the contraindications list — but with important, testable exceptions.

cards.q: CLINICAL SCENARIO 3 — During a supervisory visit to a rural health centre, you find the refrigerator holding BCG and DPT vials in the freezer compartment alongside polio and measles. The thermometer reads 2 °C. An opened DPT vial without preservative has been in use since this morning, 4 hours ago. What is wrong, and what must be done?
cards.lecturer:
**Three cold chain violations are present.**

**1. BCG and DPT are in the freezer.** Vaccines which must be stored in the **cold compartment and never allowed to freeze** are **BCG, DPT, DT, TT, typhoid and diluents**. Only **polio and measles** are stored in the freezer compartment. **The frozen BCG and DPT must be discarded** — freezing destroys them.

**2. The temperature is 2 °C.** At health centres, most vaccines (except polio) can be stored up to 5 weeks **if the refrigerator temperature is strictly kept between 4 °C and 8 °C**. 2 °C is below the acceptable range and risks freezing the non-freezable vaccines.

**3. The opened DPT vial has been in use for 4 hours.** Storage of an opened vial **without preservatives is up to 1 hour**; with preservatives, up to 3 hours. **This vial must be discarded.**

**Actions:** discard all frozen BCG and DPT and the expired opened vial; correct the refrigerator setting to 4–8 °C and monitor with a thermometer; retrain staff on which vaccines may be frozen and which may not, including that **diluents must never be frozen**; ensure vaccines are **protected from sunlight** and kept away from **antiseptics**; check the availability and condition of cold chain equipment — cold boxes, vaccine carriers, day carriers and ice packs; and identify children who may have received damaged vaccine and arrange revaccination.
cards.explain:
## Step 1: Why does this scenario matter more than it looks?

Because of the most dangerous fact about the cold chain:

>> **A damaged vaccine looks completely normal.**

---

It is the right colour. It draws up normally. It injects normally. The child gets a card saying he is immunised.

>> **He is not.** And nobody finds out until there is an outbreak.

This is why supervisory visits exist.

---

## Step 2: Violation 1 — BCG and DPT in the freezer

---

**The rule:**

| **FREEZER** | **COLD — never freeze** |
|---|---|
| **Polio** | **BCG · DPT · DT · TT · Typhoid · DILUENTS** |
| **Measles** | |

---

**Why can polio and measles be frozen?**

>> They are **live viral** vaccines. Cold preserves living organisms; **heat** is what kills them.

---

**Why must BCG and DPT never be frozen?**

>> They are protein-based — **inactivated vaccines, toxoids and adsorbed vaccines**.

**Freezing denatures the protein** — it destroys the three-dimensional shape the immune system recognises. And it damages the **adjuvant**, which is what makes adsorbed vaccines work at all.

---

>> **Once frozen, they are permanently destroyed.** Thawing does not restore them.

**Discard them. There is no way to test or rescue them.**

---

## Step 3: Violation 2 — the temperature is 2 °C

---

**The rule: strictly 4 °C to 8 °C.**

---

**Why is 2 °C wrong, when it is still above freezing?**

>> Because a refrigerator does not hold one uniform temperature.

If the average reads 2 °C, then **parts of the fridge — especially near the cooling element and at the back — are already below 0 °C.**

---

>> **Vaccines in those spots are freezing without anyone knowing.**

The 4 °C lower limit is not arbitrary. It is a **safety margin** against exactly this.

---

**And both ends of the range are dangerous:**

- **Above 8 °C** → live vaccines die
- **Below 0 °C** → protein vaccines are destroyed

**The window is narrow and unforgiving in both directions.**

---

## Step 4: Violation 3 — the opened vial at 4 hours

---

**The rule:**

| | Time |
|---|---|
| **WITH preservative** | **3 hours** |
| **WITHOUT preservative** | **1 hour** |

---

This vial has **no preservative** and has been open **4 hours**.

>> **Four times the permitted limit. Discard it.**

---

**Why is the limit so short?**

**Step 1:** Every time a needle enters the vial, **bacteria can be introduced**

**Step 2:** The vial contents are at room temperature during a session

**Step 3:** Bacteria **multiply**

**Step 4:** With no preservative, nothing slows them

---

>> **You would be injecting bacteria into a child.** This is not about vaccine potency — it is about causing infection.

That is why the no-preservative limit is only 1 hour.

---

## Step 5: What else should you check while you are there?

---

**Sunlight**

>> **UV light destroys vaccines.** BCG and measles are especially sensitive.

Are vials sitting in their boxes, or out on a bench in the sun?

---

**Antiseptics**

>> Antiseptics **kill living organisms** — which is exactly what a live vaccine is.

Is the swab allowed to dry before injection? Is antiseptic being stored beside the vials?

---

**Equipment**

Check: deep freezer, cold boxes, **vaccine carriers**, day carriers, **ice packs**.

>> Without working carriers and ice packs, **outreach immunization stops entirely**. The cold chain is what makes outreach possible.

---

**A thermometer and a temperature log**

>> If nobody is reading and recording the temperature twice daily, this will happen again next month.

---

## Step 6: The step most people forget entirely

>> **Identify the children who may have received the damaged vaccine — and arrange revaccination.**

---

**Why does this matter so much?**

Those children are:

- **Recorded as immunised**
- **Counted in the coverage figures**
- **Completely unprotected**

---

>> They are the most dangerous kind of unimmunised child, because **nobody is looking for them.**

And this is the direct link to the herd immunity card:

>> **High coverage on paper means nothing if the vaccines were dead.**

---

## Step 7: Retraining, not blame

The staff member who put DPT in the freezer was probably trying to keep it **extra cold** — thinking colder is safer.

>> That instinct is exactly backwards for protein vaccines, and it is a very common error.

**Teach the rule, label the compartments, and post the temperature range on the fridge door.**

---

## Putting it all together

>> **Only polio and measles go in the freezer. Everything else — BCG, DPT, DT, TT, typhoid and DILUENTS — must never freeze.**

>> **4–8 °C, strictly. Not colder.**

>> **Opened vial: 3 hours with preservative, 1 hour without.**

>> **And find the children who got the dead vaccine.**
cards.tie:
This case shows why the cold chain closes the lecture.

Everything before it — the right vaccine, the right age, the right child — is undone by one warm afternoon or one frozen shelf.

cards.q: KEY CUES — What words and findings should trigger a specific answer in immunization questions?
cards.lecturer: Summary of the discriminating triggers drawn across the whole lecture.
cards.explain:
## The active/passive cues

**"Immediate protection needed" · "already exposed" · "post-exposure"**

→ **PASSIVE** — preformed antibody. Works now, no memory.

---

**"Long-term protection" · "prevent future disease"**

→ **ACTIVE** — vaccine or toxoid. Slow, lasting, with memory.

---

**"Dirty wound in an unimmunised child"**

→ **BOTH** — antitoxin (passive, now) **and** toxoid (active, later).

---

**"IgG" + "placenta"** → passive **natural**, before birth

**"IgA" + "breast milk"** → passive **natural**, after birth

---

## Live vs inactivated cues

**"One dose enough" · "long lasting" · "like natural infection"**

→ **LIVE ATTENUATED**

---

**"3–5 doses" · "titres diminish" · "cannot cause disease" · "mostly humoral"**

→ **INACTIVATED**

---

**"Circulating antibody interferes"**

→ **LIVE** — it must replicate, and antibody neutralises it. Explains **measles at 9 months** and **blood transfusion as an MMR precaution**.

---

**LIVE bacteria (only two):** **BCG · typhoid**

**TOXOIDS (only two):** **diphtheria · tetanus**

**Conjugate polysaccharide:** **Hib**, pneumococcal, meningococcal

---

## Immune globulin cues

**"Pooled from many adults"** → **immune globulin** — hepatitis A, measles

**"High specific antibody, from humans"** → **hyperimmune globulin** — PEP for hep B, **rabies, tetanus**, varicella

**"From ANIMALS" · "horse serum"** → **heterologous antitoxin** — **botulism, diphtheria**

>> **Risk of anaphylaxis and serum sickness. Test first.**

**"Clone of antibody-producing cells"** → **monoclonal** — **RSV, palivizumab**

---

## The three lists — the most examined part

### CONTRAINDICATIONS → DO NOT GIVE

**Anaphylaxis** · **encephalopathy** · **PROGRESSIVE neurologic disorder** · immunodeficiency / immunosuppressive therapy · pregnancy · **illness requiring hospital admission (defer)**

---

### PRECAUTIONS → WEIGH IT UP

Fever **>40.5 °C** · collapse/shock · inconsolable crying · Guillain–Barré

**MMR only:** **blood transfusion** · **thrombocytopenia**

---

### NOT CONTRAINDICATIONS → GIVE THE VACCINE

**URTI** · **mild fever** · **diarrhoea (but repeat dose)** · **cerebral palsy** · **breastfeeding** · **antimicrobial therapy**

---

>> **The trap:** *progressive* neurologic disorder = **do not give**. **Cerebral palsy** (static) = **GIVE**.

---

## HIV cues

**"HIV, ASYMPTOMATIC"** → **ALL vaccines**

**"HIV, SYMPTOMATIC"** → **BCG and yellow fever CONTRAINDICATED**

**"HIV" (any)** → **IPV preferred to OPV** — inactivated cannot cause vaccine-associated polio

**"Immunocompromised"** → **live vaccines generally not given**

---

## Special circumstance cue

**"SCA · asplenia · nephrotic syndrome · renal failure · HIV · organ transplant"**

→ **Pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine.**

>> All share poor defence against **encapsulated** organisms.

---

## Programme cues

**"Resistance of a GROUP" · "large proportion immune"**

→ **HERD IMMUNITY.** Requires **high coverage**.

---

**"No contraindication but did not receive the vaccine he was eligible for"**

→ **MISSED OPPORTUNITY.**

**Five causes:** vaccine unavailable · **not checking immune status** · **failure to give simultaneous injections** · failure to inform parents · **failure to immunize every day**

---

**"Percentage of target population immunised" · "12–23 months"**

→ **IMMUNIZATION COVERAGE.**

---

## COLD CHAIN cues — high yield

**FREEZER:** **polio · measles** (the live viral pair)

**COLD, NEVER FREEZE:** **BCG · DPT · DT · TT · typhoid · DILUENTS**

---

**"Refrigerator temperature"** → **strictly 4 °C to 8 °C**

**"Storage at health centre"** → up to **5 weeks**, **except polio**

---

**Opened vial:**

- **WITH preservative → 3 hours**
- **WITHOUT preservative → 1 hour**

---

**"Left in sunlight"** → destroys vaccine

**"Contact with antiseptic"** → kills live vaccine

---

## Two facts examiners love

>> **Edward Jenner, 1796** — first to publish; smallpox vaccine **from cowpox virus**; the **first live vaccine**. Smallpox had **20–30% case fatality**.

>> **Smallpox eradication saved 32 billion dollars.**
cards.tie:
This is your fast-recall layer.

The three lists and the cold chain split are where most marks are won and lost — learn those two blocks cold.
